# Transcrição: 06-A Mop Sink and Maybe God

- **Fonte:** 06-A Mop Sink and Maybe God
- **Duração:** 06:39
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Thank you. I'm standing in a mop sink in a tiny closet in the police station in Bourne, Massachusetts. The door is closed and there's no light in the closet so it's pitch black. It's 1993. I'm 22 years old and I'm about to confess to a crime I didn't commit. I came to this police station two weeks ago. A $7,000 deposit disappeared at the McDonald's where I work and after three days of frantic searching my boss and I determined it was not going to be found. I was responsible for it and I accepted responsibility and said I'd pay back what was lost. She said no. She said it's company policy that you don't pay it back and it's not the first deposit that's ever gone missing so don't worry. I'll lose about a year of promotions but I'm a rising star in the McDonald's managerial career so she says you'll be fine. We have to go to the police station to report the loss for insurance purposes so that day we go and we meet a police officer. He's a tall guy. He's old. He's got steel gray hair. I tell him what happened. I say I brought three deposits to the bank and only two ended up in the night drop. I don't know where the other one went. He listens and then he says we need to go back so I can ask you some more questions. My boss jumps in and says no, no. We don't think he stole the money. We're just here to report it for insurances. He says I understand but we still need to go out back. I go to the back room with him and I'm in this tiny room with a metal table and chairs and he pushes a piece of paper across the table. It's my waiver that I need to sign, my Miranda waiver which means I'm going to be questioned without an attorney and I'm going to accept that. I do because I didn't do anything and innocent people don't need lawyers. It's what I'm thinking. I sign my Miranda rights away and he starts to question me and almost immediately he gets right to drugs. He asks what drugs do you use and are you selling? I tell him I'm not using drugs. In fact, I have never actually used an illegal drug in my entire life and he does not believe that. I want him to believe it because it is true. I've been on my own since I was 18 years old and it has been really hard for me. I don't have parents and I don't have family. I'm living without a safety net. I feel alone in this world and I made a decision when I was young that I wasn't going to use drugs because if I had a problem, no one was going to be able to save me. He doesn't believe me and I can see it in his eyes but after an hour he says you can go and as I'm leaving the police station, my boss leans in and says what the hell was that? I say I don't know but I'm just glad it's over. But it wasn't. Two weeks later the police officer calls me again and he says he needs me for more questions and so I go to the police station and I'm back in that little room with the metal table and now there's another police officer there too. He's a younger guy and he's smiling when I walk in and it gives me a cup of sprite and I like him right away.

## [03:01 - 06:01]

But when I say those words out loud and I hear them out loud, I realize how insane it is to say I did this thing and for the first time in two weeks like my body fills up with fuck you, like this is not going to fucking happen to me. ## [06:01 - 06:39]

and I'm not going to say I did. And so they arrest me and they send me to jail and it is a long two years before I'm sitting in a courtroom and I'm found not guilty. But that is not my victory day. My victory happens in that mob sink, in that dark closet. When I call out to a God that I don't really think exists and he maybe gives me the answer I needed to hear and somehow I think in that dark space that maybe I'm not alone. Thank you.
